coredata.dc:description: For environmental remediation, the development of innovative photocatalysts for efficient degradation of pollutants is essential. This paper construct three-dimensional (3D) Si-doped TiO2 (Si-TiO2) hollow material, and explores its photocatalytic reaction efficiency after composite g-C3N4 under F-doped carbon dots (F-CDs) modification. Silica opal template can be used to prepare Si-TiO2 with hollow spherical structure and layered macroscopic mesoporous structure. Due to the confinement effect of template and the formation of Si-O-Ti bonds, anatase phase Si-TiO2 hollow spheres do not undergo structural phase transition during high-temperature calcination. The incorporation of g-C3N4 can increase the light absorption range, and facilitate the formation of heterojunction with Si-TiO2, thereby improving the charge carrier generation and separation efficiency. Since F-CDs contains carbon core and abundant surface functional groups, it is favorable to increase the number of active sites and adsorb more g-C3N4, thus increasing the heterojunction area. It can also be used as the electronic medium to accelerate carrier separation and transport rate between g-C3N4/Si-TiO2 heterojunctions. Finally, thanks to the large specific surface area, abundance of active sites, excellent light absorption performance and effective charge separation and transport performance, the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 photocatalyst can decompose 3 mg L−1 of Rh B under visible light, and the degradation rate can reach 74 % in 50 min under 10 mg conditions. The insights gained from this study could provide useful information for the development of effective photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For environmental remediation, the development of innovative photocatalysts for efficient degradation of pollutants is essential. This paper construct three-dimensional (3D) Si-doped TiO2 (Si-TiO2) hollow material, and explores its photocatalytic reaction efficiency after composite g-C3N4 under F-doped carbon dots (F-CDs) modification. Silica opal template can be used to prepare Si-TiO2 with hollow spherical structure and layered macroscopic mesoporous structure. Due to the confinement effect of template and the formation of Si-O-Ti bonds, anatase phase Si-TiO2 hollow spheres do not undergo structural phase transition during high-temperature calcination. The incorporation of g-C3N4 can increase the light absorption range, and facilitate the formation of heterojunction with Si-TiO2, thereby improving the charge carrier generation and separation efficiency. Since F-CDs contains carbon core and abundant surface functional groups, it is favorable to increase the number of active sites and adsorb more g-C3N4, thus increasing the heterojunction area. It can also be used as the electronic medium to accelerate carrier separation and transport rate between g-C3N4/Si-TiO2 heterojunctions. Finally, thanks to the large specific surface area, abundance of active sites, excellent light absorption performance and effective charge separation and transport performance, the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 photocatalyst can decompose 3 mg L−1 of Rh B under visible light, and the degradation rate can reach 74 % in 50 min under 10 mg conditions. The insights gained from this study could provide useful information for the development of effective photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Various organic dyes have become one of the most common water pollutants with the development of bio-dyeing, textiles, printing and industry [1]. It is difficult to biodegrade and can cause water-borne diseases, so it has therefore attracted a great deal of attention [2,3]. Rhodamine B (Rh B) is a zwitterionic dye extensively utilized in both biological and industrial contexts, owing to its remarkable brightness, water solubility, and stability [4]. However, Rh B is extremely harmful, even low concentrations of Rh B in the exposed environment can cause cancer and reproductive and respiratory damage in humans and animals [5–7]. Therefore, it is urgent to tackle organic pollutant in wastewater. Semiconductor photocatalytic technology is regarded as a highly effective approach for pollutant remediation, attributed to its benefits such as moderate reaction conditions, superior efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendly nature [8]. Among the numerous semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has gained significant attention in recent years due to its benefits, which include affordability, non-toxicity, high stability, and a broad spectrum of degradation capabilities [9–11]. However, in practical applications, TiO2 demonstrates suboptimal photocatalytic performance primarily due to its intrinsic wide band gap (∼3.2 eV), necessitating activation by ultraviolet (UV) light, which constitutes only a minor fraction (∼4%–5%) of the solar spectrum. Additionally, its rapid recombination of photoexcitons significantly limits the availability of charge carriers.

To address the above intrinsic limitations, various strategies, including surface modification and doping, have been employed to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. Different TiO2 surface morphologies have been developed, including tubular [12], hollow spheres [13], linear [14], sheet [15], etc. They can improve charge transport performance by reducing the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Materials with hollow structures, specifically, have demonstrated significant potential. Several prior investigations [13,16] have indicated that TiO2 photocatalysts structured as hollow spheres have abundant active sites and high specific surface area, thus achieving higher photocatalytic efficiency. Moreover, incorporating trace elements as dopants is an essential strategy for enhancing photocatalytic performance. This approach can create doping energy levels within the band gap, thus extending the light absorption spectrum of TiO2. As we all know, a simple method of improving the crystallinity of materials is high temperature calcination, but anatase phase TiO2 with good catalytic effect will be irreversibly transformed into rutile phase after high-temperature treatment [17], and the photocatalytic activity will deteriorate [18]. However, numerous studies have indicated that the incorporation of certain metals (such as Cu and Al) and elements (including Si, N, and P) can impede the phase transition of TiO2 at elevated temperatures [19–21].

Despite the use of surface modification and doping to modify TiO2, its photocatalytic performance remains insufficient for effective use in real-world applications, partly due to the ineffective improvement of its light absorption performance. Coupling with other narrow-band gap semiconductor materials (MoS2, Ag3PO4, g-C3N4, Fe2O3, etc) is one of the effective strategies to break through the bottleneck of TiO2 [22–25].Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), recognized as a prominent metal-free semiconductor, has garnered significant interest within the photocatalysis community due to its cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness and narrow band gap. The research indicates that incorporating g-C3N4 decreases the band gap of g-C3N4/TiO2 composites and broadens their optical response, enabling activity within the visible light spectrum [26–28]. Nonetheless, the photocatalytic performance remains limited due to the swift recombination of photoexcited carriers and the sluggish rate of interfacial charge transfer [29]. Consequently, the development of photocatalysts that exhibit efficient light absorption and enhanced charge transport properties is both necessary and promising.

Carbon-based materials, particularly carbon dots (CDs) with dimensions under 10 nm, have garnered significant interest owing to their affordability, environmental compatibility, abundant surface functional groups, and electron transfer characteristics [30,31]. Due to their unique upconversion photoluminescence properties and abundant surface active sites, CDs are often combined with a wide range of semiconductors and serve as electron reservoirs, improving the photoresponse and photogenerated charges transport and separation performance of composite materials [32]. In addition, the strong chemical interaction between CDs and other semiconductors can also make the material more stable [33]. Therefore, the application of CDs in TiO2 and g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst, and further observing their effects on heterojunction quality, light absorption capacity and charge transport performance, as well as their impact on photocatalytic performance, will be a very meaningful topic. However, there are currently few reports on this research.

In this research, we constructed a composite material consisting of F-doped CDs (F-CDs) modified Si-doped TiO2 (Si-TiO2) hollow spheres combined with g-C3N4, and investigated its photocatalytic degradation performance of Rh B. The three-dimensional (3D) anatase phase Si-TiO2, characterized by its hollow spherical structure and layered macroscopic mesoporous architecture, can be synthesized utilizing silica opal templates. The influence of Si incorporation on the crystalline phase, structural characteristics, and charge transport behavior of TiO2 was examined. In addition, the influence of introducing F-CDs as electronic media on the light absorption range, carrier transport and photocatalytic properties of TiO2/g-C3N4 heterojunction was studied. Importantly, the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material with the optimal content significantly improves the photocatalytic efficiency in contrast to the pristine TiO2 and the F-CDs/TiO2 composites. Simultaneously, a comprehensive investigation is conducted into the correlation between Si doping, F-CDs modification, and the inherent characteristics of the g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunction, along with an in-depth analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism.

The following reagents were obtained from commercial sources: tetraethyl orthosilicate, ammonium hydroxide, tetrabutyl titanate, anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %), acetonitrile, PVP, sodium hydroxide, urea, ammonium fluoride, melamine. All reagents are analytical grade reagents and can be used directly without further purification. In this experiment, ultrapure water resistance ≥18.2 MΩ cm (Shanghai Shenshen ultrapure water Machine UPTA-1-10) was used.

The Si-doped TiO2 hollow spheres were prepared using silica opal template, and a few modifications were made. Firstly, 23.6 mL of ethanol and 0.6 mL of deionized water were added to a beaker. Then, 0.6 mL of ammonium hydroxide and 0.86 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were added to the solution, followed by stirring magnetically for 6 h. The resulting milky solution was centrifuged and washed sequentially with water and ethanol to obtain the SiO2 nanosphere templates. These templates were then suspended in 40 mL of ethanol. The mixture A was prepared by adding 14 mL of acetonitrile and 0.4 mL of ammonium hydroxide to the SiO2 suspension. Simultaneously, in another beaker, mixture B was prepared by adding 6 mL of ethanol, 2 mL of acetonitrile and 0.8 mL of tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT). During the stirring process, the mixture B was carefully added to the mixture A and stirred for 3 h to obtain TiO2-coated SiO2 spheres. The resulting milky solution was centrifuged, and the precipitate was washed sequentially with ethanol and water to obtain white TiO2/SiO2 powder. The powder was dispersed in 40 mL of water, and 0.4 g PVP was added. The mixture was stirred for 12 h to adsorb PVP on the surface of TiO2. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged, and the powder was collected.

Add 46 mL of ethanol and 8.6 mL of deionized water to a beaker. The TiO2/SiO2 powder was added to the mixture and ultrasonically dispersed. Then,1.2 mL of ammonium hydroxide and 1.6 mL of TEOS were added to the suspension, followed by stirring for 4 h to obtain the SiO2/TiO2/SiO2 product. The product was centrifuged, and then washed sequentially with water and ethanol. Afterward, it was dried at 80 °C for 3 h. The dried powder was ground into fine particles and then annealed in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 2 °C·min−1 to 900 °C for 2 h.

Etching of SiO2 Templates: The annealed product was dispersed in 80 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution, and stirred in a preheated oil bath at 90 °C for 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h and 2 h, respectively. Then the product was washed with water and ethanol in turn and dried at 80 °C for 3 h. Finally, the Si-TiO2 hollow spheres with different etching times were obtained.

Dissolve 0.3 mg urea (CH4N2O) and 0.092 mg ammonium fluoride (NH4F) in 30 mL of deionized water. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated at 120 °C for 6 h, and naturally cooled to obtain F-CDs.

Weigh 1 g melamine and transfer it to a crucible. Then, it was annealed in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1 to 550 °C and maintained this temperature for 4 h. The resulting yellow product was ground into powder in a mortar to obtain g-C3N4.

Take 30 mg of Si-TiO2 and add it to 30 mL of water. Then, take different amounts (10, 20 and 30 mg) of F-CDs and add them to the above solution. Ultrasound for 5 min to achieve better dispersion, then stir at room temperature for 2 h. Afterward, the F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material was obtained by centrifugation and drying. The samples containing different amounts (10, 20 and 30 mg) of F-CDs were named as follows: F-CDs/Si-TiO2-(x) (where x = 1, 2, 3).

First, 80 mg F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material and 20 mL of deionized water were added to a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and stirred for 20 min. Simultaneously, different amounts (13.5, 27 and 40.5 mg) of g-C3N4 were added to 20 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 20 min. Then, add this solution to the polytetrafluoroethylene liner, and continue stirring for 20 min. Finally, the polytetrafluoroethylene liner was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave and carry out a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the materials was centrifuged with water and ethanol, and dried in an oven to obtain the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material. The photocatalytic composite materials prepared with different amounts (13.5, 27 and 40.5 mg) of g-C3N4 were named as follows: g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2-(y) (where y = 1, 2, 3).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted on a Bruker D8 Advance instrument with a scanning speed of 5°·min−1 using Cu Kα radiation over the range of 5°–80°. SEM and TEM were utilized to analyze the morphology and microstructure of the material. The Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer records Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi + spectrometer. Surface area and pore size distribution were measured using a 3 Flex full-function physisorption analyzer. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600Plus). J-V curves and Mott-Schottky plots were recorded using a CHI 660E three-electrode electrochemical workstation.

The visible light source is simulated by 500 W xenon lamp. The photocatalytic process was conducted on a multi-position photochemical reactor of the CEL-LAB500 series photolysis apparatus. Simply put, 10 mg photocatalyst was poured into 100 mL of Rh B aqueous solution (3 mg L−1). To reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium before illumination, magnetically stirring was performed in the dark for 30 min. During the photocatalytic degradation process, 5 mL of the suspension was extracted every 10 min, and the supernatant was collected after centrifugation. The absorbance spectra of the supernatant were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To further evaluate the reusability of the photocatalyst, similar photocatalytic tests were conducted consecutively for five cycles. In the radical scavenging experiment, 1 mM of IPA, EDTA and BQ were added to 100 mL Rh B solution respectively as scavengers for ·OH, h+ and O2 − radicals for catalytic sampling.

Fig. 1 illustrates the synthesis process. In contrast to many previous studies which utilized polymer sphere templates to prepare 3D TiO2 hollow spheres [34–37], this study employed SiO2 as the templates, which serve three functions during the synthesis process: acting as the hard templates for forming hollow spherical structures, acting as the suppressors to inhibit the growth of TiO2 grains and restrict phase transformation, and acting as the dopant to facilitate the incorporation of surface-level Si doping into TiO2 structure. When the silica opal templates are immersed in the TiO2 solution, a layer of amorphous TiO2 will grow on their surface. Then a layer of SiO2 is regenerated on the surface of TiO2 to form a SiO2/TiO2/SiO2 structure. During this process, the hydroxyl groups (─OH) on the surface of SiO2 nanospheres form hydrogen bonds and Si-O-Ti bonds with TiO2, thereby enhancing the interfacial bonding between the two materials. In the subsequent high-temperature calcination process, amorphous TiO2 transforms into interconnected anatase nanocrystals due to Si-O-Ti bonds and nanospace confinement, but does not transform into the rutile phase. It can be seen that the uniqueness of silica opal templates lie in its strong inhibitory effect on crystal growth and phase transition. In addition, hydroxyl groups from SiO2 will form hydrogen and Si-O-Ti bonds with TiO2, and the Ti-OH and Si-OH bonds can be crosslinked by the Si-O-Ti bonds, allowing Si to dope into the lattice of TiO2 to occupy lattice positions. After removing the template, some Si bonds remain in the sample to form Si-TiO2. The Si doping can generate hydroxyl groups (─OH) on the surface of TiO2, which is conducive to adsorbing F-CDs and to photocatalytic reaction. The F-CDs are composed of carbon cores and numerous surface functional groups, which is conducive to the composite with g-C3N4, and eventually form g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite photocatalytic material.

SEM and TEM were used to study the morphology of the prepared material. The TEM image of un-etched SiO2/TiO2/SiO2 is shown in Fig. S1(a), indicating that the sample has a spherical structure approximately 470 nm in diameter. The light gray regions in the middle and outermost layers are composed of SiO2, while the black region between them represents TiO2. From Fig. S1(b), it can be observed that the diameter of F-CDs is approximately 2–5 nm, and they are well dispersed without aggregation. The SEM image (Fig. S2(a)) shows that Si-TiO2 consists of nanoparticles forming a spherical structure approximately 320 nm in diameter. In Fig. S2(b), the Si-TiO2 nanospheres exhibit a hollow morphology with an estimated wall thickness of approximately 80 nm. This structural feature enhances the material's specific surface area and boosts the concentration of active sites. Fig. S2(c, d) show the SEM and TEM images of F-CDs/Si-TiO2, respectively. It can be observed that after composite F-CDs, the F-CDs/Si-TiO2 still have a hollow spherical structure composed of nanoparticles, but the diameter of the spheres has increased to about 360 nm. Fig. 2 (a–c) show the SEM and TEM images of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2. It can be seen that due to the irregular g-C3N4 nanoparticles covering the surface of F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material, the diameter of the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 hollow spheres is increased (∼400 nm), which is slightly larger than that of the F-CDs/Si-TiO2 samples. Fig. 2 (d) shows the HRTEM image of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 hollow sphere, where the lattice spacings of 0.35 nm, 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm correspond to the {101} crystal plane of TiO2, {100} crystal plane of CDs and the {002} crystal plane of g-C3N4, respectively, demonstrating the successful embedding of CDs and the formation of heterojunction between g-C3N4 and TiO2. Fig. 2(e) depict the elemental mapping spectra of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2, revealing the uniform distribution of Ti, Si, O, F, C, and N elements, which further confirms the successful preparation of the composite photocatalyst.

Fig. 3 (a) displays the XRD spectra of Si-TiO2, F-CDs, g-C3N4, and their composite materials. In the analysis of the Si-TiO2 spectrum, it is evident that the produced TiO2 exhibits the characteristic structure of anatase, corresponding well with the standard card. The diffraction peaks are attributed to the (101), (004), (200), (105) and (204) crystal planes of anatase phase TiO2. According to literature reports, TiO2 typically undergoes a 600–700 °C phase transition from anatase to rutile, primarily driven by grain growth [38]. When the larger anatase grains reach a state of thermodynamic instability, they convert into the rutile phase, thereby reducing the surface free energy. In this experiment, TiO2 did not transform into rutile phase structure after 900 °C high temperature annealing. This is because the silica opal template has a nanospace confinement effect during calcination, which can inhibit the growth of TiO2. Meanwhile, the presence of Si-O-Ti bonds at the SiO2/TiO2/SiO2 interface acts as an additional barrier, impeding the enlargement of the grains. In addition, the absence of a distinctive SiO2 peak in the spectrum confirms that Si is incorporated as a dopant within the TiO2 hollow sphere structure. The spectrum of F-CDs shows a broad peak near 2θ = 24°, corresponding to the (002) diffraction plane, indicating that F-CDs have been successfully prepared. The diffraction peaks of the prepared g-C3N4 at 2θ = 13.1° and 27.4° correspond to the (100) and (002) crystal planes, respectively, confirming the successful preparation of g-C3N4. Only the diffraction peak of Si-TiO2 is observed in the F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material, without the peak of F-CDs, which is caused by the low content of F-CDs. The XRD spectrum of the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material exhibits characteristic peaks of TiO2 and g-C3N4 without the emergence of new diffraction peaks. This indicates that no new substances are produced during the material compounding process. The positions of diffraction peaks associated with g-C3N4 remain unchanged, indicating that the crystal structure of g-C3N4 remains unaltered after being combined with F-CDs/Si-TiO2. The above results demonstrate the successful preparation of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite materials.

Chemical bond and functional group analysis was conducted using FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The absorption bands near 450 cm−1 for Si-TiO2, F-CDs/Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 are associated with the Ti-O-Ti bonds. A peak observed around 960 cm−1 is associated with the Ti-O-Si bond, which further demonstrates the successful doping of Si in TiO2. In F-CDs/Si-TiO2, the band observed at 1402 cm−1 corresponds to the C-O vibrational mode, while the distinctive absorption peak at 1256 cm−1 indicates the presence of the C-F bond, thereby confirming the incorporation of CDs within the composite structure. Additionally, the spectral band spanning 1567-1626 cm−1 is linked to the stretching and bending vibrations of ─OH groups present in both Si-TiO2 and CDs. The absorption of water appears as a wide spectral band spanning the range of 3000–3800 cm−1. This further proves the existence of abundant surface ─OH in the samples, which can provide abundant adsorption and reaction sites, thereby promoting photocatalytic reactions. In the infrared spectrum of g-C3N4, the absorption peak observed at 810 cm−1 is indicative of the triazine ring's vibrational mode, while the peaks located at 1240, 1317, 1408, and 1638 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations associated with the carbon-nitrogen ring structure. Additionally, the peak at 3280 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the ─NH2 group and ─OH bond. The infrared spectrum of the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 sample clearly exhibits characteristic peaks of Si-TiO2, F-CDs, and g-C3N4, without the appearance of any new peaks. Moreover, abundant surface functional groups are favorable for photocatalytic reactions.

The surface electronic properties of the samples were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full-scan spectrum depicted in Fig. 3(c) confirms the presence of several elements, including Si, Ti, O, F, C and N in the ternary composite material, which aligns with the previously discussed elemental mapping outcomes. This finding provides additional evidence for the existence of F-CDs and g-C3N4 within the material. Fig. 3(d–i) display the high-resolution XPS spectra of individual elements in the composite material. In Fig. 3(d), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Ti 2p peaks in Si-TiO2 shows that their binding energies are 459.37 eV and 465.03 eV, respectively. In g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2, the Ti 2p peaks appear at binding energies of 459.29 eV and 464.95 eV, which are typical for the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks of TiO2, indicating that the Ti element in the sample is in the +4 oxidation state. As shown in Fig. 3(e), the XPS spectra of O 1s reveal that both Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 materials exhibit three characteristic peaks at 530.5 eV, 532.13 eV and 533.28 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies and oxygen absorption, respectively. Compared to Si-TiO2 material, the proportion of oxygen vacancies decreases with the addition of CDs and g-C3N4 in g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2. The reason for this is that after compounding with CDs and g-C3N4, the oxygen vacancies are filled by the material and the ratio decreases. The lattice oxygen ratio is elevated. Fig. 3(f) shows the XPS spectrum of Si 2p. The binding energies of Si 2p in Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 materials are 102.99 eV and 103.05 eV, respectively. The findings suggest that silicon is present in the +4 oxidation state. In Fig. 3(g), the XPS analysis of the F 1s spectrum reveals two prominent peaks, corresponding to the covalent C-F bond and the semi-ionic C-F bond, respectively. These peaks indicate the presence of C-F bonds in the samples, further confirming the formation of F-CDs. The XPS spectrum of C 1s, as illustrated in Fig. 3(h), displays distinct peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, 289.05 eV, and 290.01 eV. These peaks are indicative of the presence of C-C, O=C-O, C-N, and C-F bonds, respectively. The findings verify the existence of F-CDs and g-C3N4. In Fig. 3(i), the N 1s spectrum of the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite is illustrated, showing distinctive peaks at 399.35 eV, 400.37 eV, and 402.22 eV. These peaks correspond to the C-N=C bond, the N-(C)3 bond, and the C-N-H bond, respectively, thereby confirming the presence of g-C3N4.

The structure characteristics of as-prepared materials were studied by analyzing the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution curves. To investigate the effect of the addition of F-CDs and g-C3N4 on the specific surface area and pore structure of Si-TiO2 hollow spheres, BET analysis was conducted. As shown in Fig. S3, the Si-TiO2 hollow spheres and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite materials present the type IV adsorption isotherm, confirming the mesoporous structures of the products. Furthermore, the specific surface area of pure Si-TiO2 is 207.39 m2 g−1, which is higher than many other TiO2 samples prepared by high-temperature calcination processes [39–42]. And the specific surface area of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 is 183.4 m2 g−1, indicating that compared with Si-TiO2, the specific surface area of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 is slightly reduced, mainly due to the partial filling of the pores of Si-TiO2 by F-CDs and g-C3N4 nanoparticles. Tthe pore-size distribution curves show that the introduction of F-CDs and g-C3N4 increases the number of pores with a diameter of over 5 nm, which facilitates the flow of liquid into the pores and further promotes the photocatalytic reaction. Although the specific surface area of TiO2 is reduced, the change of photocatalytic performance is the combined effect of light absorption capacity, active site and charge transport. Therefore the effects of the content of Si doping, the incorporation of CDs and the addition of g-C3N4 were investigated afterward.

In order to fully understand the degradation properties of the prepared materials, the degradation performance of Rh B was compared among Si-TiO2 with different etching times, different F-CDs loading amounts, and different g-C3N4 composite levels. As illustrated in Fig. S4(a), the effect of varying etching durations of Si-TiO2 on the photocatalytic breakdown of Rh B is depicted. Over a period of 1.5 h, the efficiency of Rh B degradation progressively increased as the degradation time extended. This is because with the increase of etching time, SiO2 content decreases and a hollow spherical shape is formed, which increases the light scattering path of TiO2 and can improve the light absorption performance. Moreover, TiO2 hollow spherical morphology can increase the specific surface area, which helps provide more active sites. In addition, the etching time also affects the amount of Si doping. When the Si doping concentration reaches the optimum, more surface hydroxyl groups will be generated to promote the photocatalytic reaction. When the etching time increased to 2 h, the degradation efficiency begins to decline. This may be due to the reduction of Si doping content, resulting in a corresponding decrease in surface hydroxyl groups and active sites.

Based on the above experiments, we chose the Si-TiO2 sample with an etching time of 1.5 h and added F-CDs to enhance the photocatalytic performance. Fig. S4(b) shows the degradation curve of Rh B after the addition of F-CDs. As the content of F-CDs increases, the CDs function as both electron mediators and acceptors, enhancing the efficiency of photogenerated charge carrier migration and thus boosting photocatalytic performance. However, when an excess amount of F-CDs is added, aggregation occurs, reducing the active sites. Additionally, the CDs lose their multi-exciton effect and quantum confinement effect after aggregation, resulting in a decrease in the degradation performance of the photocatalyst.

Based on the best-performing F-CDs (2)/Si-TiO2 sample, we constructed heterojunctions by combining different amounts of g-C3N4. Since g-C3N4 has a smaller bandgap compared with TiO2 and less energy is required for electron leaps, the heterojunction formed between TiO2 and g-C3N4 can effectively promote the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers and inhibit the recombination of carriers. In Fig. S4(c), as the content of g-C3N4 increases, the degradation efficiency of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material gradually improves. This is because the area of the heterojunction between g-C3N4 and TiO2 gradually increases, further improvement of the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. However, excessive addition of g-C3N4 will cause agglomeration, which is unfavorable to the charge transfer, thus reducing the photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic degradation performance of different catalysts within 50 min is shown in Fig. 4 (a) . It can be observed that the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material achieves a degradation efficiency of approximately 74 % within 50 min using only 10 mg of catalyst to degrade a 3 mg L−1 Rh B solution. In order to get a clearer picture of the photocatalytic performance of samples, the apparent reaction rate constant k (min−1) for pseudo-first-order kinetics is determined using the following equation:
(1)
− ln
(
C ∕
C 0
)
= k t
where k is the reaction rate constant, and C and C 0 represent the solution concentration of Rh B at time t and the original solution concentration. The data depicted in Fig. 4(b) indicates that the pseudo-first-order kinetic curves align with the principles governing photodegradation. The g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 sample exhibits a k value of 0.0265, which is approximately double that of the pure Si-TiO2 sample and nearly fivefold higher than that of the pure g-C3N4 sample. This is because F-CDs is composed of carbon cores and abundant surface functional groups, which can not only accelerate the migration rate of photogenerated carriers, but also provide a large number of active sites, which is conducive to the load of g-C3N4, enhancing both light absorption and the quality of the heterojunction. Consequently, this process fosters the efficient generation and separation of photogenerated carriers, ultimately boosting the photocatalytic performance. Compared to the degradation of Rh B by other photocatalysts (Table S1) [43–51], the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composites demonstrate excellent degradation performance.

To investigate the reactive species involved in photocatalytic processes, IPA, EDTA and BQ were added to the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2--(2) system as scavengers for ·OH, h+ and ·O2 −, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(c), after adding different scavengers, the degradation rate is reduced to varying degrees compared to the photocatalytic system without catalyst, indicating that h+, ·OH and ·O2-are all the core active species for degradation. Among them, the effectiveness of active species is ·OH > ·O2 − > h+. The durability of the photocatalyst under degradation conditions is essential for its practical use. As shown in Fig. 4(d), after 5 cycles of reuse, the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material has no significant changes, demonstrating the excellent stability of this composite material.

The UV–Vis light absorption test is used to investigate the light absorption properties of as prepared materials, as shown in Fig. 4(e). Compared to Si-TiO2, F-CDs/Si-TiO2 exhibits a stronger response, indicating that the addition of F-CDs significantly enhances the light absorption capability. The g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material exhibits a redshift in the edge of light absorption, which broadens the light absorption spectrum of the composites. This indicates that the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material can acquire more visible light.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing was conducted to explore the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges. As depicted in Fig. 4(f), SiO2/TiO2/SiO2 exhibits the largest radius size, indicating its significant resistance to charge transfer. Compared to Si-TiO2, the F-CDs/Si-TiO2 sample demonstrates a smaller radius size. Moreover, the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material exhibits the smallest radius size among the samples. This indicates that the construction of the heterojunction between g-C3N4 and Si-TiO2, as well as the introduction of F-CDs, both contribute to reducing the resistance of charge transfer, facilitating the separation and transfer of charges. Because of the light absorption and charge transfer properties of the samples, the J-V curves were tested. As shown in Fig. S5, consistent with the trend observed in EIS test results, the photocurrent density of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composites is significantly higher than that of F-CDs/Si-TiO2 and Si-TiO2 materials. These findings suggest that integrating F-CDs and g-C3N4 leads to an increase in the number of photogenerated carriers and enhances charge transport properties. This improvement is advantageous for boosting the photocatalytic efficiency of the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite materials.

To better understand the charge transfer pathways on the heterojunction structure of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2, the bandgap and conduction band (CB) position of TiO2 and g-C3N4 were determined using light absorption and Mott-Schottky tests, as shown in Fig. S6. The energy gap between the CB and valence band (VB) of the synthesized semiconductor photocatalysts was determined by analyzing the relationship between absorbance and photon energy, as expressed by the following equation:
(2)
a h ν = A
(
h ν −
E g
)
n
where E g denotes the band gap and n is a constant, α, A and hv correspond to the absorption coefficient, proportional coefficient and photon energy. In Figs. S6(a and b), the bandgap width of TiO2 and g-C3N4 are 3.14 eV and 2.75 eV, respectively. In addition, from the Mott-Schottky curves of Figs. S6(c and d), it can be seen that the slopes are both positive, indicating that Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4 are typical n-type semiconductors, and their CB positions are −0.44 eV and −0.97 eV, respectively. We can also use the following formula to calculate the VB position of the materials.
(3)
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g
In which, E VB represents the VB potential, E CB represents the CB potential, and E g denotes the bandgap width. Through calculation, the VB position of TiO2 and g-C3N4 are determined to be 2.7 eV and 1.78 eV, respectively. Therefore, we can finally obtain the band position diagram of the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 composite material to investigate the degradation mechanism of the materials studied in this paper.

The electronic properties and interface mechanisms of the prepared materials were investigated using density functional theory (DFT) and density of states (DOS) analysis. Firstly, the structures of Si-TiO2, g-C3N4, F-CDs/Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 were simulated. In Figs. S7(a and b), the model diagrams of Si-TiO2 with (101) surface and g-C3N4 with (002) surface are presented respectively. According to the HRTEM images, it can be observed that the (101) surface of TiO2 forms a well-defined heterojunction with the (002) surface of g-C3N4. Fig. 5 (a, b) shows the models of F-CDs/Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 incorporating CDs. To further investigate the electronic transfer behavior at the interfaces, the charge density difference maps of F-CDs/Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 materials were constructed, as shown in Fig. 5(c and d). It can be seen from Fig. 5(c) that there is electron transfer at the interface between CDs and Si-TiO2. The light blue areas indicate electron depletion from Si-TiO2, while the yellow areas indicate electron accumulation on CDs, demonstrating that the CDs act as electron acceptors at the interface and confirming electron migration from Si-TiO2 to CDs. When F-CDs/Si-TiO2 is analyzed as a whole, it can be observed from Fig. 5(d) that the electron transfer occurs at the interface between g-C3N4 and F-CDs/Si-TiO2. The CDs/Si-TiO2 shows a tendency towards electron depletion, while at the interface with g-C3N4, there is an accumulation of electrons, indicating that the electrons are transferred from F-CDs/Si-TiO2 to g-C3N4. It is consistent with the Z-type heterojunction proposed in the experiment. As shown in Fig. 5(e and f), the charge density plots clearly reveal that upon addition of CDs and g-C3N4, there is an increase in charge density at their interface, forming protrusions around this region. This indicates a changed charge distribution in the composite. The gradient from blue to red in the figures represents the gradual change in electron density from depletion to accumulation [52]. The above results theoretically prove the transfer of electrons.

In order to understand the mechanism behind the enhanced performance of g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 materials, we calculated the total density of states (TDOS). Interestingly, high electron density of states (DOS) values at the Fermi level are considered crucial evidence of superior photocatalytic activity [53]. Fig. 5(g, h) show the DOS plots for Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4, respectively. Compared to Si-TiO2, the g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 material exhibits higher DOS and a smaller band gap near the Fermi level, as shown in Fig. 5(i). This indicates its enhanced electron transfer capability and accelerated charge transfer kinetics. These results confirm that the heterojunction formed between Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4, along with the CDs acting as electron mediators and electron acceptors, can overcome the activation energy barriers, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the material.

Building upon the previous experimental findings, a potential photocatalytic mechanism for g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 is further proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , upon exposure to visible light, both Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4 facilitate the generation of electron-hole pairs. F-CDs, as the electron mediators, accelerate the rate of charge carrier transfer. Meanwhile, the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes is facilitated by the heterojunction formed by Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4. If the energy band arrangement between g-C3N4 and Si-TiO2 forms a type II heterojunction. Compared with Si-TiO2, the CB of g-C3N4 is more negative. Electrons transfer from the CB of g-C3N4 to that of Si-TiO2. At the same time, the VB of Si-TiO2 is more positive compared to that of g-C3N4, so holes can be transferred from the VB of Si-TiO2 to that of g-C3N4. However, the VB potential of g-C3N4 is lower than that of H2O/·OH (2.38 V), indicating that the holes in g-C3N4 cannot oxidize H2O to ·OH [54]. The experimental conditions are not satisfactory. Therefore, the scheme of type II heterojunction is incorrect, based on which the scheme of type Z heterojunction is proposed. Under light excitation, the e− in the CB of Si-TiO2 is easily combined with h+ in the VB of g-C3N4 due to the strong electrostatic attraction, retaining e− in the more negative CB of g-C3N4 and h+ in the more positive VB of Si-TiO2. In this way, the holes on VB of Si-TiO2 can oxidize H2O to produce ·OH, and the electrons in CB of g-C3N4 can be trapped by dissolved O2 to form ·O2 −. On the one hand, photogenerated electrons, as the primary active species, with their strong reduction ability, can directly participate in the degradation of Rh B. On the other hand, the electrons on CB react with O2 to form O2 − active species, enhancing the degradation efficiency of Rh B by the photocatalyst. Meanwhile, the holes on VB react with H2O to generate ·OH active species, which also play a crucial role in the degradation of Rh B. The above results indicate that the construction of heterojunction between Si-TiO2 and g-C3N4, along with the addition of F-CDs, can effectively enhance the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges, increase the amount of active species, thereby significantly improving the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst.

In summary, Si-doped TiO2 hollow spheres were successfully prepared using a template method, and a novel catalyst with high catalytic performance was synthesized by incorporating F-CDs and subsequently constructing a heterojunction with g-C3N4. The silica opal template can not only serve as a source of Si doping in TiO2, but also suppress the structural phase transition of anatase phase TiO2 during high-temperature calcination through its confinement effect and Si-O-Ti bonds. The incorporation of g-C3N4 improves the catalyst's utilization of visible light and facilitates the formation of a heterojunction with Si-TiO2, accelerating electron transfer and enhancing the lifetime of photogenerated charges. The addition of F-CDs accelerate the transfer rate of carriers, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The prepared g-C3N4/F-CDs/Si-TiO2 catalyst exhibits strong light harvesting capability, rapid electron transfer rate and excellent photocatalytic activity. In this study, we greatly improved the light absorption capacity of TiO2 and generated more surface active groups to participate in the photocatalytic degradation reaction based on the establishment of heterojunctions by constructing a three-dimensional hollow sphere morphology of TiO2, Si doping modification of TiO2 and addition of F-CDs, which provided an effective strategy for the preparation of high performance TiO2-based photocatalysts. It is expected to provide new insights for the design of high-performance photocatalysts.
